note: Tightened the estimator class to measurable estimators with pointwise values in [-M,M] and made the n-fold product law explicit.
Added the explicit case split on which error probability attains the maximum before transferring the event-integral bounds to the MSE maximum.

problems.detail: A pair of bounded outcome channels is constructed so that the regression functions differ by a localized Hölder bump in the treatment coordinate. → The lower bound is presented through a two-point Le Cam construction that perturbs the treatment regression.

The bump has bandwidth \(h\asymp n^{-1/(2\alpha+1)}\), producing separation of order \(h^\alpha\) in \(\theta_P(t_0)\), while the Kullback--Leibler divergence of the \(n\)-sample experiments remains bounded. → The construction yields the lower-bound rate \(n^{-2\alpha/(2\alpha+1)}\) through the certified two-point Le Cam argument.

The slack condition ensures that these alternatives stay inside the same boundedness, positivity, and Hölder restrictions. → The theorem assumes the strict baseline slack condition to obtain the stated same-class lower bound.

kennedy2017: The evidence places Kennedy et al. in continuous-treatment causal estimation but does not specifically state that it formulates consistency as observed outcome equaling the realized-dose potential outcome. — "Consistency is the standard requirement that the observed outcome equals the potential outcome at the realized exposure "

bonvini2022: The evidence says Bonvini and Kennedy study dose-response rates and higher-order estimation, but it does not mention a bounded-outcome or uniform envelope condition. — "The bounded-outcome condition is a standard uniform envelope restriction \citep{bonvini2022}."

colangelo2020: The evidence identifies Colangelo and Lee as related continuous-treatment DDML work, but it does not specifically mention an interior evaluation point condition or boundary avoidance. — "This is the standard interior evaluation point condition for continuous-exposure dose-response estimation \citep{colange"

bonvini2022: The evidence links Bonvini and Kennedy to local and global dose-response estimation, but it does not specifically state that they impose local positivity or overlap near the target dose. — "\]



Local positivity is the standard overlap requirement near the target dose \citep{bonvini2022}."

bonvini2022: The brief says Bonvini and Kennedy study dose-response estimation under anisotropic Holder structure, but it does not specifically establish that the Holder-smooth covariate density condition is described there as a standard density regularity assumption. — "The Hölder-smooth covariate density condition is a standard density regularity assumption \citep{bonvini2022}."

stone1982: Stone 1982 is identified as establishing optimal global nonparametric regression rates, while the sentence attributes a one-dimensional pointwise regression exponent to it. — "The exponent in Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:sharp-pointwise-lower-bound} is the one-dimensional nonparametric pointwise regress"

bonvini2022: The brief supports that Bonvini and Kennedy (2022) develops a higher-order influence-function estimator under tailored smoothness/regularity structure, but it does not specifically verify the claim about localized products of nuisance errors. — "This distinction matters because the higher-order influence-function literature often works with localized products of n"

bonvini2024: The provided evidence establishes this characterization for Bonvini and Kennedy 2022, but is silent on whether Bonvini et al. 2024 specifically uses localized products of nuisance errors or additional estimator-tailored regularity structures. — "This distinction matters because the higher-order influence-function literature often works with localized products of n"

stone1982: The sentence says the case is d≤4s, but the displayed lemma assumes the deficient-covariate regime 4s<d, so the attribution context is internally inconsistent even though Stone is a classical rate reference. — "It says that, when \(d\le 4s\), the comparison benchmark reduces to the one-dimensional treatment-regression exponent, t"

tsybakov2009: The sentence says the case is d≤4s, but the displayed lemma assumes 4s<d, so the cited context overreaches or mismatches the stated regime despite Tsybakov being a standard nonparametric reference. — "It says that, when \(d\le 4s\), the comparison benchmark reduces to the one-dimensional treatment-regression exponent, t"

Local positivity is the standard overlap requirement near the target dose \citep{bonvini2022}."

bonvini2024: The provided evidence establishes this characterization for Bonvini and Kennedy 2022, but is silent on whether Bonvini et al. 2024 specifically uses localized products of nuisance errors or additional estimator-tailored regularity structures. — "This distinction matters because the higher-order influence-function literature often works with localized products of n"

weaknesses: The central theorem is named 'Sharp Pointwise Lower Bound' (Thm thm:sharp-pointwise-lower-bound) but sharpness is never established on the same class — the only matching upper rate is cited for a different (Bonvini–Kennedy localized-regularity) class. The name overclaims; rename to something like 'Treatment-Direction Minimax Floor' and state explicitly that same-class sharpness is open even when d<=4s.

Novelty is under-articulated: the floor n^{-2a/(2a+1)} is the classical Stone (1982) one-dimensional pointwise regression rate, and the construction (Lemma oracle-dose-regression-lower-all-beta) holds the covariate density fixed at a constant baseline, so the covariate-averaging structure plays no role. The Introduction/Discussion should state plainly what this adds beyond invoking Stone — i.e., that it is a 'no-faster-than-1D' floor embedded in the continuous-treatment class — rather than presenting it as a substantive new bound.

def:beta-frontier-handle and oeq:full-beta-frontier package interpretive prose and an open question inside 'Definition' environments (the latter even labeled 'oeq'). These are not definitions; recast them as a Remark and an explicit Open Problem so the reader is not led to expect new estimands.

The same disclaimers — 'this is a lower bound not an upper bound' and 'the Bonvini–Kennedy result is for a different class' — are repeated roughly eight times (Abstract, Introduction, Main results, each theorem's trailing paragraph, Discussion, and the Verification note). Consolidate to one clearly-placed statement in the Introduction and one in the Discussion to tighten the register.

The benchmark rho_n is attributed to Bonvini–Kennedy but citedv lem:published-upper-bound-cited gives no precise pointer (theorem number, exact class definition) and reuses the symbol R_n — which Definition def:minimax-risk fixes to the Hölder class — for risk over the external class. Fix the notation collision (use a distinct symbol for the external risk) and give an exact citation to the published rate and its class.

A single constant M is used simultaneously as the outcome envelope (ass:bounded-outcome), every treatment- and covariate-Hölder ball radius (ass:mu/pi/px-holder), and the covariate-density upper bound (ass:px-holder); these are different physical scales. The assumption section should either justify collapsing them or introduce separate constants, since this conflation also makes Assumption baseline-submodel-slack (norms <= M - eta_0 across heterogeneous objects) harder to interpret.

The model-class definition includes causal assumptions and iid sampling as properties of single-observation laws, which is conceptually awkward and can blur the minimax object. Suggested fix: in Section 2, separate the statistical model for observed laws from the causal interpretation overlay, and define the minimax class only over observed-data restrictions unless the lower bound truly requires potential-outcome structure.

The baseline slack assumption is central but under-discussed relative to its strength. Suggested fix: after Assumption baseline-submodel-slack, add a short paragraph explaining when such slack baselines exist, whether it imposes restrictions on M, c0, and beta beyond positivity, and whether the theorem is conditional on nonemptiness of the class.

Several statements use strong language like “Sharp Pointwise Lower Bound,” “Full beta frontier,” and “Smooth Covariate Minimax Floor,” although the paper proves only a lower bound and explicitly leaves same-class upper rates unresolved. Suggested fix: rename these to “Pointwise Lower Bound,” “Beta-Uniform Lower Floor,” and “Smooth-Covariate Lower Floor,” or add explicit caveats in the theorem headings and first sentences.

The cited Bonvini--Kennedy comparator is handled conservatively in prose, but the displayed statement “R_n <= C rho_n” in the cited result lacks a defined model class and could be read as applying to the paper’s R_n. Suggested fix: introduce separate notation, e.g. R_n^{BK}, for the external localized-regularity class and use that notation throughout the comparator lemma.

The appendix proof architecture contains too much verification scaffolding and Lean-oriented narration for a working paper, which weakens readability. Suggested fix: move Lean comments, object labels, and step-by-step formal proof bookkeeping into a separate verification appendix or artifact note, and keep the econometrics appendix focused on the construction and statistical argument.

The assumption discussion motivates standard smoothness and positivity one by one, but it does not adequately discuss the unusual asymmetry of smoothness requirements, especially that only x -> mu(t0,x) and x -> pi(t0|x) are constrained rather than joint smoothness. Suggested fix: add a paragraph clarifying why pointwise-in-dose covariate smoothness suffices for the stated lower bound and how this differs from the regularity used in the HOIF upper-bound literature.

problems.detail: bonvini2024: The supplied evidence does not describe Bonvini et al. 2024 or connect it to any of the listed recent-work categories. — "Recent work studies modified treatment policies, weak-overlap or alternative identification regimes, nonparametric infer"

bonvini2022: The evidence identifies Bonvini and Kennedy 2022 as a dose-response estimation benchmark but does not mention bounded outcomes or common-envelope testing reductions. — "Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:bounded-outcome} is the standard uniform bounded outcome condition; it keeps the loss and testin"

colangelo2020: The evidence says Colangelo and Lee develop continuous-treatment partial-mean/DDML analyses, but it is silent on an interior evaluation point or boundary-effect condition. — "\]



Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:interior-dose} is the standard interior evaluation point condition, excluding boundary eff"

bonvini2022: The evidence links Bonvini and Kennedy 2022 to interior dose-response estimation, but it does not specifically establish a local positivity assumption. — "\]



Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:local-positivity} is the standard local positivity condition; it requires enough treatment"

bonvini2022: The evidence says Bonvini and Kennedy 2022 studies dose-response estimation under smoothness structure, but does not specifically verify Holder smoothness of the conditional treatment density. — "Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:pi-treatment-holder} is the standard Hölder smoothness condition for the conditional treatment d"

bonvini2022: The evidence supports Bonvini--Kennedy as involving covariate smoothness, but it is silent on a marginal covariate density Holder-ball assumption with a uniform upper bound. — "Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:px-holder} is the standard Hölder-smooth covariate density condition, together with a uniform up"

kennedy2017: The evidence identifies Kennedy et al. as a direct antecedent for continuous-treatment causal estimation, but it does not specifically verify that it states the consistency condition. — "Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:consistency} is the standard consistency condition, linking the observed outcome to the potentia"

bonvini2024: The supplied evidence does not describe Bonvini et al. (2024), so it is silent on whether that work emphasizes the stated continuous-exposure or overlap-sensitive issues. — "Recent work on continuous exposures and overlap-sensitive causal estimands emphasizes related issues from complementary "

bonvini2022: The evidence supports that Bonvini and Kennedy provide the motivating HOIF upper-rate benchmark, but it does not verify the narrower claim that their result is not a same-class upper bound for the paper's specific Holder dose class. — "The Bonvini--Kennedy upper-side comparator in \ref{obj:lem:published-upper-bound-cited} is imported from \citet{bonvini2"

weaknesses: The flagship Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:sharp-pointwise-lower-bound} is titled "Sharp Pointwise Lower Bound" and Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:oracle-dose-regression-lower-all-beta} is the "All-Beta Oracle Floor," yet the paper repeatedly and correctly admits no matching same-class upper bound is proved; "Sharp" (and to a lesser extent "oracle/minimax floor") asserts a tightness the paper disclaims. Fix: rename to something like "Treatment-Regression Lower Floor" and reserve "sharp" for the smooth-covariate regime where the exponent provably matches the benchmark.

The lower bound n^{-2\alpha/(2\alpha+1)} is just the classical one-dimensional nonparametric-regression rate, obtained by perturbing only \mu_P and effectively ignoring the covariate dimension (the construction in Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:oracle-dose-regression-lower-all-beta} embeds a 1D problem). The introduction frames this as a substantive difficulty result without stating plainly that the rate's origin is classical and that the genuine contribution is the constraint-preserving embedding (keeping alternatives in-class while respecting local positivity, density smoothness, and the causal overlay). Fix: add one sentence in the introduction crediting the 1D obstruction as classical and locating novelty precisely in the embedding.

The deficient regime (4s<d), where the certified in-class lower-floor exponent is *strictly larger* than the published benchmark exponent \rho_n, is the paper's most informative finding but is presented defensively rather than analyzed: a valid lower bound exceeding a published upper bound (on the Bonvini-Kennedy localized class) directly demonstrates the two classes are genuinely different and that the Hölder class is strictly harder. Fix: in the discussion of Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:frontier-bracket-deficient}, state explicitly that this exponent gap *proves* non-inclusion of the Hölder class in the localized-regularity class (or otherwise forbids transporting the HOIF upper bound), turning a caveat into a positive conclusion.

The scope caveat ("a lower bound, not an upper bound, not a complete minimax characterization") is repeated 15+ times across the abstract, introduction, every main-result remark, and the appendices, which reads as insecurity and obscures the actual content; the two closing Remarks (\ref{obj:def:beta-frontier-handle} and \ref{obj:oeq:full-beta-frontier}) are near-verbatim duplicates and Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:certified-beta-frontier-bracket} restates the three main theorems. Fix: consolidate the scope disclaimer into one paragraph in the introduction, delete one of the two final Remarks, and demote the bracket lemma to a one-line pointer.

The class imposes \beta-Hölder smoothness on the treatment density (Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:pi-treatment-holder}) and carries \beta in all notation, yet the lower bound is \beta-insensitive at the exponent level and Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:baseline-submodel-slack} is engineered precisely to keep \pi fixed so \beta never binds. The paper notes the insensitivity but never discusses why \beta belongs in the class definition or whether the slack baseline is generically satisfiable. Fix: add a short paragraph on the restrictiveness/feasibility of Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:baseline-submodel-slack} and on what role (if any) \beta could play in an upper bound, so the reader understands why it is retained.

Definition~\ref{obj:def:holder-dose-class} (the Hölder dose class) opens by referencing the minimax risk and benchmark \rho_n "as in Definitions~\ref{obj:def:minimax-risk} and~\ref{obj:def:published-hoif-rate}," which are defined later and on which the class does not actually depend, creating an apparent circular/forward dependency. Fix: remove these forward references from the class definition; the class is purely a set of laws and needs neither the risk nor the benchmark to be well-defined.

Main results repeatedly use labels such as “Sharp Pointwise Lower Bound,” “Smooth Covariate Minimax Floor,” and “frontier” despite the paper not proving a same-class upper bound; revise titles and nearby prose to say “treatment-regression lower floor” or “same-class lower bound” unless a matching upper result is supplied.

In the cited HOIF comparator, the display still says `R_n \le C\rho_n` after introducing `\bar R_n^{\mathrm{BK}}`; replace the display with `\bar R_n^{\mathrm{BK}} \le C\rho_n` and state the exact Bonvini--Kennedy class assumptions being imported.

The baseline-submodel slack assumption is central but under-discussed; after Assumption 11, add a paragraph explaining when such `p_0,q_0,\eta_0` exist, what restrictions it imposes on `M,c_0,\varepsilon_0`, and whether common examples satisfy it.

The causal overlay is included in the model class although the lower bound is an observed-data statement; clarify in Definition 12 and the proof of Lemma 18 that the constructed two-point observed laws admit potential-outcome extensions satisfying consistency and ignorability, or else keep the causal assumptions outside the statistical class.

The paper is very defensive about not proving a same-class upper bound, but the repetition is excessive and sometimes obscures the contribution; consolidate these caveats in the abstract, after Definition 14, and in the discussion, and shorten repeated qualifications in theorem commentary.

The appendix proof of Lemma 18 invokes several named devices such as the bump Hölder gate, doseWitness membership, and KL tensorization without enough paper-level detail; add a short explicit construction of the bump `\psi`, its support/normalization, and how it preserves each class restriction so readers need not rely on Lean labels.

problems.detail: bonvini2024: The provided evidence for Bonvini et al. 2024 gives only a title about doubly robust inference and optimality in smoothness models, without enough detail to verify the specific related-work categories in the sentence. — "Recent work studies modified treatment policies, weak-overlap or alternative identification regimes, nonparametric infer"

bonvini2022: The displayed assumption is about the marginal covariate density, while the sentence attributes a conditional treatment-density smoothness condition to the cited work. — "\cref{obj:ass:pi-covariate-holder} imposes the standard covariate smoothness condition on the conditional treatment dens"

kennedy2017: Kennedy et al. is identified as a direct continuous-treatment causal antecedent, but the evidence provided is silent on whether it specifically states the consistency condition in this form. — "\cref{obj:ass:consistency} is the standard consistency condition, linking the observed outcome to the potential outcome "

bonvini2024: The brief identifies Bonvini and Kennedy 2022 as the closest upper-bound paper, but provides no specific evidence about Bonvini et al. 2024 and its relation to continuous exposures or overlap-sensitive causal estimands. — "Recent work on continuous exposures and overlap-sensitive causal estimands emphasizes these complementary regimes \citep"

goldenshluger2020: The evidence supports Goldenshluger--Lepski as a lower-bound reference for anisotropic smoothness and nonlinear density functionals, but it is silent on a treatment-coordinate local testing construction specialized to an interior partial-mean functional. — "The construction is the usual local nonparametric testing argument in the treatment coordinate \citep{stone1982,tsybakov"

bonvini2024: The provided evidence for Bonvini et al. 2024 gives only a title about doubly robust inference and optimality in smoothness models, without enough detail to verify the specific related-work categories in the sentence. — "Recent work studies modified treatment policies, weak-overlap or alternative identification regimes, nonparametric infer"

bonvini2022: The evidence identifies Bonvini and Kennedy 2022 as a close dose-response upper-bound paper, but it is silent on whether it specifically supports the uniform bounded outcome envelope condition. — "\cref{obj:ass:bounded-outcome} is the standard uniform bounded outcome condition; it keeps the loss and testing reductio"

kennedy2017: Kennedy et al. is identified as a direct continuous-treatment causal antecedent, but the evidence provided is silent on whether it specifically states the consistency condition in this form. — "\cref{obj:ass:consistency} is the standard consistency condition, linking the observed outcome to the potential outcome "

bonvini2024: The brief identifies Bonvini and Kennedy 2022 as the closest upper-bound paper, but provides no specific evidence about Bonvini et al. 2024 and its relation to continuous exposures or overlap-sensitive causal estimands. — "Recent work on continuous exposures and overlap-sensitive causal estimands emphasizes these complementary regimes \citep"

goldenshluger2020: The evidence supports Goldenshluger--Lepski as a lower-bound reference for anisotropic smoothness and nonlinear density functionals, but it is silent on a treatment-coordinate local testing construction specialized to an interior partial-mean functional. — "The construction is the usual local nonparametric testing argument in the treatment coordinate \citep{stone1982,tsybakov"

weaknesses: The paper never demonstrates that \cref{obj:ass:baseline-submodel-slack} is satisfiable, yet every main result is conditional on it. Since the condition requires a covariate density with Hölder norm at most M-\eta_0, a treatment density bounded below by c_0+\eta_0 on the window and Hölder-bounded by M-\eta_0, the reader cannot tell whether the theorem set is nonempty for small M or large c_0. Add a short lemma or worked example in Section 2 (e.g. p_0 uniform on [0,1]^d, q_0 uniform on [0,1]) exhibiting explicit (\eta_0, M, c_0) ranges for which the condition holds, and state the parameter restriction it induces.

The assumption block in Section 2 attaches a one-sentence 'this is the standard X condition' gloss with a citation to each of \cref{obj:ass:bounded-outcome}--\cref{obj:ass:px-holder}, but never discusses restrictiveness or what fails without them. Notably \cref{obj:ass:local-positivity} imposes positivity uniformly over ALL x\in[0,1]^d, which is substantially stronger than the local overlap conditions typically assumed and is exactly what makes the lower-bound construction easy; and \cref{obj:ass:bounded-outcome} shares its constant M with the Hölder radius and density bound, an artificial coupling. Add a paragraph after \cref{obj:ass:px-holder} discussing which assumptions bind the construction, and either decouple M into separate constants or justify the coupling.

The gloss on \cref{obj:ass:pi-covariate-holder} reads 'imposes the standard covariate smoothness condition on the marginal covariate density', but the assumption is about x\mapsto\pi_P(t_0\mid x), the conditional treatment density at the target dose, not the marginal covariate density (that is \cref{obj:ass:px-holder}). Rewrite the sentence to describe the propensity surface at the evaluation dose.

The paper's central positioning claim -- that \citet{bonvini2022} Theorem 3.1 holds on a strictly smaller class than \cref{obj:def:holder-dose-class} -- rests entirely on the reconstruction inside \cref{obj:lem:published-upper-bound-cited}, where the decisive extra condition (higher covariate-density smoothness s_p\ge s making the third-order remainder negligible) is attributed to a '\S3.4 discussion' rather than a stated condition. Because the entire contribution is framed as a same-class/other-class separation, this needs to be tightened: state the exact inequality on s_p under which the remainder is negligible, or explicitly flag that the subclass is the authors' reading of an informal discussion and that the separation is conditional on that reading.

\cref{obj:def:beta-frontier-handle} and \cref{obj:oeq:full-beta-frontier} are near-verbatim restatements of each other, both restating the lower floor and the two regime identities already given in \cref{obj:thm:sharp-minimax-smooth-covariate} and \cref{obj:thm:frontier-bracket-deficient}, and they introduce two symbols (\mathfrak B_{\alpha,\beta,s}, \mathfrak Q_{\alpha,\beta,s}) that are used nowhere else. Delete one remark and fold the surviving content into a single short paragraph closing Section 3, dropping the unused notation.

The abstract and introduction assert that the exponent 'holds for every fixed \beta>0', but the quantifier order is the weaker one: for each \beta a separate slack baseline and constant are chosen, and feasibility of \cref{obj:ass:baseline-submodel-slack} may itself depend on \beta. The Section 4 paragraph acknowledges this, but the abstract's phrasing invites a uniform-in-\beta reading. Rewrite the abstract clause as 'for each fixed \beta>0 admitting a slack baseline, with a constant depending on \beta'.

The interior-window condition is stated in three mutually non-identical forms -- \cref{obj:ass:interior-dose} says [t_0-\varepsilon_0,t_0+\varepsilon_0]\subset(0,1); \cref{obj:thm:sharp-pointwise-lower-bound} says 'the local window determined by (t_0,\varepsilon_0) is contained in (0,1)'; \cref{obj:lem:oracle-dose-regression-lower-all-beta} says 'the window of radius \varepsilon_0 around t_0 lies in the interior dose region'. Standardize on the displayed set inclusion in all three, and note whether \varepsilon_0<1/2 is implied by or additional to it.

note: Added the Lean-required nonnegativity/range condition on the cube.
Added the Lean-required outcome scale B_0 with 0<B_0<M and made the density nonnegativity and normalization clauses explicit.
Removed the unformalized Le Cam construction and restored the eventual-in-n lower floor, all n>=1 rate identities, exponent comparison, and the Lean disjunction of upper-frontier alternatives.
Tightened to the estimator-specific eventual conditional-MSE implication and inlined the BK theorem and rate-specialization hypotheses.
Added the three Lean semantic-tie membership conditions and made the covariate-density range content explicit.
